questions.scenario: You need to run three commands grouped together as a unit, in a subshell, and capture the combined exit code.
questions.question: Run 'echo start && echo middle && echo end' in a subshell using parentheses.
questions.description: ( ) runs commands in a subshell — a child process. Variables set inside don't affect the parent. Working directory changes don't persist. The subshell's exit code is the last command's exit code.
use_cases: Group commands for combined exit code checking
Run commands in a different directory without affecting the parent
hints: Put the commands inside with && between them
Run: (echo start && echo middle && echo end)

questions.scenario: You set a variable inside a subshell. Prove that it does NOT persist in the parent shell.
questions.question: Set X=changed inside a subshell, then echo $X outside it. Show X is still empty in the parent.
questions.description: Subshells inherit the parent's environment but cannot modify it. Any variable assignment inside ( ) is invisible to the parent. This is a critical distinction — use export or command substitution to pass values out.
use_cases: Safely test variable changes without affecting the running script

questions.scenario: You need to cd into /tmp to run a command, but you must return to the original directory afterward — without using pushd/popd.
questions.question: Change into /tmp inside a subshell, print the working directory, and confirm the parent shell is still in /home/student.
questions.description: cd inside a subshell only changes the working directory for that child process. The parent shell is completely unaffected. This is the cleanest way to run commands in a different directory temporarily.
use_cases: Run a build command in a specific directory without affecting caller
Execute scripts that expect a certain CWD
Keep working directory clean in deploy scripts

questions.scenario: You need to capture the output of a group of commands into a variable.
questions.question: Capture the output of (echo 'reactor' && echo 'online') into a variable RESULT and print it.
questions.description: $( ) is command substitution — it runs a command (or subshell) and captures stdout as a string. The subshell inside $() follows the same isolation rules: variables set inside don't affect the parent.
use_cases: Capture multi-command output into a variable
Build dynamic strings from command output
Store timestamps, hostnames, or other runtime values
hints: Wrap the subshell in $() to capture output

questions.scenario: Each command in a pipeline runs in its own subshell. Prove this by showing a variable set inside a while-read loop doesn't persist.
questions.question: Echo 'test' into a pipe to a while loop that sets VAR=set, then print $VAR outside — show it's empty.
questions.description: In bash, each element of a pipeline runs in a subshell by default. This is a notorious gotcha: variables set inside a while-read pipeline loop are NOT visible after the pipe. Use process substitution or lastpipe option as workarounds.
use_cases: Debug 'why is my loop variable empty' bugs
Know when to use process substitution instead of pipes
hints: echo 'test' | while read line; do VAR=set; done
Then echo $VAR — it should be empty
Run: echo 'test' | while read line; do VAR=set; done; echo "VAR=$VAR"

validation.command: echo 'test' | while read line; do VAR=set; done; echo "VAR=$VAR"
